hints: {a1} Formula: **be supposed to + base verb**. Usage: we use it for rules or expected behavior. Markers: rule, expected, supposed to. Example: *You'd better not aren't supposed to use this product without protection.*.

a1.are not meant to use: ❌ «are not meant to use» hints at purpose, but the sentence refers to an external rule. Example: *You'd better not aren't supposed to use this product without protection.*.
a1.aren't supposed to use: ✅ «aren't supposed to use» shows a rule or expected action. Example: *You'd better not aren't supposed to use this product without protection.*.
a1.must not use: ❌ «must not use» means a strict prohibition, while here it emphasizes a rule/expectation. Correct example: *You'd better not aren't supposed to use this product without protection.*.
a1.shouldn't use: ❌ «shouldn't use» is just advice, not a requirement of the rule. See the correct version: *You'd better not aren't supposed to use this product without protection.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **do/does not have to or needn't + base verb**. Usage: when the action is not mandatory in the present/general time. Markers: no need, optional, don't have to. Example: *You don't have to go to the ceremony if you don’t feel like it. It’ll be very boring anyway.*.

a1.mustn't: ❌ «mustn't» expresses obligation or prohibition, but we need to show that the action is optional. Correct example: *You don't have to go to the ceremony if you don’t feel like it. It’ll be very boring anyway.*.
a1.don't have to: ✅ «don't have to» means there is no obligation. Example: *You don't have to go to the ceremony if you don’t feel like it. It’ll be very boring anyway.*.
a1.haven't to: ❌ This option does not remove the obligation. Correct example: *You don't have to go to the ceremony if you don’t feel like it. It’ll be very boring anyway.*.
a1.needn't: ❌ This option does not remove the obligation. Correct example: *You don't have to go to the ceremony if you don’t feel like it. It’ll be very boring anyway.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **needn't have + V3**. Usage: indicates that the action was unnecessary but occurred. Markers: already, unnecessary action, regret. Example: *We took too much risk. We needn't have made that decision.*.

a1.needn't have made: ✅ «needn't have made» emphasizes that the action in the past was unnecessary. Example: *We took too much risk. We needn't have made that decision.*.
a1.shouldn't make: ❌ «shouldn't make» gives general advice, not that the action was already unnecessary. Correct example: *We took too much risk. We needn't have made that decision.*.
a1.mustn't have made: ❌ The form does not show that the action was pointless. See: *We took too much risk. We needn't have made that decision.*.
a1.shouldn't have made: ❌ The form does not show that the action was pointless. See: *We took too much risk. We needn't have made that decision.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **didn't need to / didn't have to + base verb**. Usage: when there was no necessity in the past. Markers: yesterday, last, past, no need. Example: *He didn't need to take the bus because his brother picked him up at the station.*.

a1.needn't have taken: ❌ «needn't have taken» means that the action did occur, although it was unnecessary. The sentence states that it was not done at all. Example: *He didn't need to take the bus because his brother picked him up at the station.*.
a1.shouldn't have taken: ❌ «shouldn't have taken» is a reproach, not a lack of obligation. See the correct example: *He didn't need to take the bus because his brother picked him up at the station.*.
a1.didn't need to take: ✅ «didn't need to take» shows that doing something was unnecessary. Example: *He didn't need to take the bus because his brother picked him up at the station.*.
a1.mustn't have taken: ❌ The option does not show that the action was optional. See: *He didn't need to take the bus because his brother picked him up at the station.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **shouldn't have + V3**. Usage: we express reproach regarding a past action. Markers: mistake, regret, past action. Example: *He shouldn't have spent so much money on the trip last summer.*.

a1.shouldn't spend: ❌ «shouldn't spend» only advises for the future, but we need to express reproach for the past. Example: *He shouldn't have spent so much money on the trip last summer.*.
a1.had better not spend: ❌ The form does not convey regret. Check the example: *He shouldn't have spent so much money on the trip last summer.*.
a1.shouldn't have spent: ✅ «shouldn't have spent» expresses reproach regarding a past action. Example: *He shouldn't have spent so much money on the trip last summer.*.
a1.mustn't have spent: ❌ The form does not convey regret. Check the example: *He shouldn't have spent so much money on the trip last summer.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be allowed/permitted to + base verb**. Usage: we talk about permissions or prohibitions. Markers: rule, regulation, allowed. Example: *The refugees are not allowed to work outside the camp.*.

a1.had better not: ❌ «had better not» sounds like advice, but we need to refer to a rule. Example: *The refugees are not allowed to work outside the camp.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ «mustn't» prohibits absolutely, but the context is about an administrative prohibition with allowed. Check: *The refugees are not allowed to work outside the camp.*.
a1.are not allowed to: ✅ «are not allowed to» clearly indicates prohibition. Example: *The refugees are not allowed to work outside the camp.*.
a1.are allowed to: ❌ «are allowed to» permits the action, but the sentence is about prohibition. See: *The refugees are not allowed to work outside the camp.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **had to / needed to + base verb**. Usage: we explain a forced action in the past. Markers: had to, needed, obligation. Example: *We couldn’t find a hotel so we had to sleep in the car. It was so uncomfortable!.*

a1.must have slept: ❌ "must have slept" means assumption, not a real necessity. Correct example: *We couldn’t find a hotel so we had to sleep in the car. It was so uncomfortable!*.
a1.had to sleep: ✅ "had to sleep" describes a forced obligation. Example: *We couldn’t find a hotel so we had to sleep in the car. It was so uncomfortable!*.
a1.should have slept: ❌ "should have slept" advises, but does not show a forced action. See: *We couldn’t find a hotel so we had to sleep in the car. It was so uncomfortable!*.
a1.would have slept: ❌ The form does not show that the action was forced. Example: *We couldn’t find a hotel so we had to sleep in the car. It was so uncomfortable!*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **mustn't + base verb**. Usage: strict prohibition. Markers: safety, strong rule. Example: *You mustn't forget to take your medicine.*.

a1.mustn't: ✅ "mustn't" strictly prohibits the action. Example: *You mustn't forget to take your medicine.*.
a1.don't have to: ❌ The form does not warn against the action. Example: *You mustn't forget to take your medicine.*.
a1.must: ❌ "must" does not contain a prohibition. See the correct variant: *You mustn't forget to take your medicine.*.
a1.ought not to: ❌ The form does not warn against the action. Example: *You mustn't forget to take your medicine.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **didn't need to / didn't have to + base verb**. Usage: when there was no necessity in the past. Markers: yesterday, last, past, no need. Example: *We didn't have to get up early because it was a holiday, so we slept till late.*.

a1.didn't have to: ✅ "didn't have to" shows that doing something was not necessary. Example: *We didn't have to get up early because it was a holiday, so we slept till late.*.
a1.needn't get up: ❌ The variant does not show that the action was optional. See: *We didn't have to get up early because it was a holiday, so we slept till late.*.
a1.needn't have got up: ❌ "needn't have got up" means that the action did happen, although it was not needed. The sentence states that it was not done at all. Example: *We didn't have to get up early because it was a holiday, so we slept till late.*.
a1.didn't need to: ❌ The variant does not show that the action was optional. See: *We didn't have to get up early because it was a holiday, so we slept till late.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **was/were supposed to + base verb**. Usage: talking about expectations in the past. Markers: supposed to, meant to, expectation in the past. Example: *You were meant to call me as soon as you arrived. I was very worried!*.

a1.were meant to: ✅ "were meant to" shows a rule or expected action. Example: *You were meant to call me as soon as you arrived. I was very worried!*.
a1.were supposed to: ❌ This form does not convey the required rule. Check the correct example: *You were meant to call me as soon as you arrived. I was very worried!*.
a1.must: ❌ "must" talks about personal requirement, not an established rule. See the example: *You were meant to call me as soon as you arrived. I was very worried!*.
a1.had to: ❌ This form does not convey the required rule. Check the correct example: *You were meant to call me as soon as you arrived. I was very worried!*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **needn't (have) + base verb**. Usage: politely saying that the gift or action was not needed. Markers: gift, politeness, no need. Example: *It’s beautiful! Thanks, but you needn't have bought anything for me.*.

a1.needn't buy: ✅ "needn't buy" politely says that the gift was not obligatory. Example: *It’s beautiful! Thanks, but you needn't buy anything for me.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **will have to / need to + base verb**. Usage: when the action will become necessary in the future. Markers: future plan, obligation. Example: *We’ll have to be more careful about what we say in the future.*.

a1.need to: ✅ "need to" shows future need. Example: *We’ll need to be more careful about what we say in the future.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should / ought to + base verb**. Usage: giving advice or soft necessity. Markers: advice, recommendation. Example: *You need see a specialist to check that knee.*.

a1.ought to: ✅ "ought to" sounds like strong advice. Example: *You ought to see a specialist to check that knee.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **won't have to / won't need to + base verb**. Usage: when the obligation will disappear in the future. Markers: future, relief, no obligation. Example: *When you get a new employee, you won't have to work so hard any longer.*.

a1.won't need to: ✅ Correct answer. Example: *When you get a new employee, you won't need to work so hard any longer.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be supposed/meant to + base verb**. Usage: we explain the purpose or normal course of events. Markers: meant to, supposed to, expectation. Example: *The effects are meant to last 6 to 8 hours.*.

a1.are supposed to: ✅ "are supposed to" indicates a rule or expected action. Example: *The effects are supposed to last 6 to 8 hours.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should / ought to have + V3**. Usage: expectation in the past that was not fulfilled. Markers: should have, regret, expectation. Example: *They ought to have offered us a discount because we couldn’t sleep in the room that we had booked.*.

a1.should have offered: ✅ "should have offered" indicates an expected but unfulfilled action. Example: *They should have offered us a discount because we couldn’t sleep in the room that we had booked.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be supposed to + base verb**. Usage: we use it for rules or expected behavior. Markers: rule, expected, supposed to. Example: *If we aren't meant to have midnight snacks, why is there a light in the fridge?*.

a1.aren't supposed: ✅ "aren't supposed" indicates a rule or expected action. Example: *If we aren't supposed to have midnight snacks, why is there a light in the fridge?*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **had to / were supposed to + base verb**. Usage: rules or duties in the past. Markers: school rules, duty in the past. Example: *When I was at school, we had to wear a uniform.*.

a1.were supposed to: ✅ "were supposed to" describes school rules. Example: *When I was at school, we were supposed to wear a uniform.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **do/does not have to or needn't + base verb**. Usage: when the action is not mandatory in the present/general time. Markers: no need, optional, don't have to. Example: *We needn't do the dishes today; we can leave them for tomorrow.*.

a1.don't need to: ✅ "don't need to" means there is no obligation. Example: *We don't need to do the dishes today; we can leave them for tomorrow.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be allowed/permitted to + base verb**. Usage: we talk about permissions or prohibitions. Markers: rule, regulation, allowed. Example: *Visitors aren't permitted to stay at night.*.

a1.aren't allowed to: ✅ "aren't allowed to" clearly indicates a prohibition. Example: *Visitors aren't allowed to stay at night.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be allowed/permitted to + base verb**. Usage: we talk about permissions or prohibitions. Markers: rule, regulation, allowed. Example: *TEACHER: What are you doing here? You are not permitted to be here.*.

a1.permitted: ✅ "permitted" clearly indicates a prohibition. Example: *TEACHER: What are you doing here? You are not permitted to be here.*.
a1.invited: ❌ The option does not convey the prohibition from the rules. Example: *TEACHER: What are you doing here? You are not permitted to be here.*.
a1.supposed: ❌ The option does not convey the prohibition from the rules. Example: *TEACHER: What are you doing here? You are not permitted to be here.*.
a1.expected: ❌ The option does not convey the prohibition from the rules. Example: *TEACHER: What are you doing here? You are not permitted to be here.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **be allowed/permitted to + base verb**. Usage: we talk about permissions or prohibitions. Markers: rule, regulation, allowed. Example: *STUDENT: Sorry, I know students aren't allowed to not go into the staffroom, but I really need to speak with Mr. Donovan.*.

a1.must: ❌ The option does not convey the prohibition from the rules. Example: *STUDENT: Sorry, I know students aren't allowed to not go into the staffroom, but I really need to speak with Mr. Donovan.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ "mustn't" absolutely prohibits, but the context is about administrative prohibition with allowed. Check: *STUDENT: Sorry, I know students aren't allowed to not go into the staffroom, but I really need to speak with Mr. Donovan.*.
a1.don't have to: ❌ The option does not convey the prohibition from the rules. Example: *STUDENT: Sorry, I know students aren't allowed to not go into the staffroom, but I really need to speak with Mr. Donovan.*.
a1.aren't allowed to: ✅ "aren't allowed to" clearly indicates a prohibition. Example: *STUDENT: Sorry, I know students aren't allowed to not go into the staffroom, but I really need to speak with Mr. Donovan.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **needn't have + V3**. Usage: we indicate that the action was unnecessary but occurred. Markers: already, unnecessary action, regret. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you needn't have come in here to talk to him.*.

a1.needn't: ✅ "needn't" emphasizes that the action in the past was unnecessary. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you needn't have come in here to talk to him.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ The form does not show that the action was unnecessary. See: *TEACHER: Well, you needn't have come in here to talk to him.*.
a1.shouldn't: ❌ "shouldn't" refers to general advice, not that the action was already unnecessary. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you needn't have come in here to talk to him.*.
a1.couldn't: ❌ The form does not show that the action was unnecessary. See: *TEACHER: Well, you needn't have come in here to talk to him.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should / ought to have + V3**. Usage: expectation in the past that was not fulfilled. Markers: should have, regret, expectation. Example: *It wasn’t really necessary. You could have gone to the principal’s office and asked him to call Mr. Donovan. Actually, that’s what you should have done.*.

a1.should: ✅ "should" indicates an expected but unfulfilled action. Example: *It wasn’t really necessary. You could have gone to the principal’s office and asked him to call Mr. Donovan. Actually, that’s what you should have done.*.
a1.ought: ❌ "ought" without have does not create the past tense. Example: *It wasn’t really necessary. You could have gone to the principal’s office and asked him to call Mr. Donovan. Actually, that’s what you should have done.*.
a1.had better: ❌ "had better" is grammatically incorrect in the past. See the correct version: *It wasn’t really necessary. You could have gone to the principal’s office and asked him to call Mr. Donovan. Actually, that’s what you should have done.*.
a1.ought to: ❌ "ought to" without have does not create the past tense. Example: *It wasn’t really necessary. You could have gone to the principal’s office and asked him to call Mr. Donovan. Actually, that’s what you should have done.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **had to / needed to + base verb**. Usage: explaining a forced action in the past. Markers: had to, needed, obligation. Example: *STUDENT: I’m sorry, I wasn’t thinking straight. I really needed to talk to him urgently.*.

a1.had: ❌ The form does not show that the action was forced. Example: *STUDENT: I’m sorry, I wasn’t thinking straight. I really needed to talk to him urgently.*.
a1.ought: ❌ The form does not show that the action was forced. Example: *STUDENT: I’m sorry, I wasn’t thinking straight. I really needed to talk to him urgently.*.
a1.needed: ✅ "needed" describes a forced obligation. Example: *STUDENT: I’m sorry, I wasn’t thinking straight. I really needed to talk to him urgently.*.
a1.must: ❌ The form does not show that the action was forced. Example: *STUDENT: I’m sorry, I wasn’t thinking straight. I really needed to talk to him urgently.*.

hints:
{a1} Formula: **had better + base verb**. Usage: strict advice with a warning about consequences. Markers: warning, better, advice. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
{a2} Formula: **do/does not have to or needn't + base verb**. Usage: when the action is not required in the present/general tense. Markers: no need, optional, don't have to. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.

a1.better: ✅ The form sounds like a convincing piece of advice. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a1.ought to: ❌ The option does not convey urgent advice. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ The option does not convey urgent advice. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a1.needn't: ❌ The option does not convey urgent advice. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.

a2.need: ✅ "need" forms the construction "don't need to", which removes the obligation. Example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a2.have: ❌ This option does not remove the obligation. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a2.must: ❌ "must" expresses obligation or prohibition, while we need to show that the action is not required. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.
a2.ought: ❌ This option does not remove the obligation. Correct example: *TEACHER: Well, you’d better think straight the next time, OK? Now, if you want to talk to Mr. Donovan, you don’t need to go to the principal’s office.*.

hints:
{a1} Formula: **have to / must + base verb**. Usage: describes present obligation. Markers: duty, must, have to. Example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
{a2} Formula: **be allowed/permitted**. Usage: describes the status of permission or prohibition without infinitive. Markers: allowed, permitted, forbidden. Example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.

a1.have to: ✅ The form conveys necessity. Example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a1.don't have to: ❌ «don't have to» removes obligation, but we need to emphasize it. Look: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a1.had better: ❌ The option does not sound like an obligation. Correct example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a1.must: ❌ The option does not sound like an obligation. Correct example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.

a2.allowed: ✅ «allowed» forms a natural collocation with the word not. Example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a2.welcome: ❌ «welcome» does not convey prohibition. Correct example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a2.encouraged: ❌ «encouraged» does not convey prohibition. Correct example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.
a2.required: ❌ «required» does not convey prohibition. Correct example: *And, please, you have to take that chewing-gum out of your mouth. You know that chewing-gum is not allowed on school premises.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **had better + base verb**. Usage: strict advice with a warning about consequences. Markers: warning, better, advice. Example: *You had better not do it again, or I’ll have to send you to detention.*.

a1.'d: ✅ «'d» conveys strong advice with a warning. Example: *You 'd better not do it again, or I’ll have to send you to detention.*.
